projectMeta.trainingType: Self-paced market readiness program for international founders

targetAudience.priorKnowledge: Business operations experience in home country; no U.S. market experience required

courseStructure.title: U.S. Market Readiness Program
courseStructure.description: A self-paced program preparing international entrepreneurs to launch and operate a business in the United States, with a focus on the Central Florida ecosystem. Covers legal setup, taxes, banking, IP protection, hiring, go-to-market strategy, business culture, and local resources.
learningOutcomes: Compare LLC, C-Corp, and S-Corp structures and identify which entity type fits their business goals and immigration status
Describe the step-by-step process for registering a Florida business through SunBiz, obtaining an EIN, and establishing a registered address
Explain U.S. federal and state tax obligations, sales tax requirements, and realistic accounting expectations for foreign-owned entities
Identify the documentation, physical presence requirements, and common rejection reasons for opening a U.S. business bank account
Distinguish between U.S. intellectual property protections (copyright, trademark, patent) and assess when professional legal counsel is needed
Analyze the differences between contractor and employee classifications, visa pathway options, and employer compliance obligations
Apply U.S. customer acquisition strategies, pricing expectations, and market entry approaches to their specific business context
Explain key U.S. business cultural norms including communication style, meeting etiquette, negotiation patterns, and trust-building signals
Identify Central Florida ecosystem resources (SBDC, SBA, CFITO, Hispanic Chamber, UCF BIP phases 2 and 3) and match them to their business needs
Assess whether U.S. market expansion is realistic for their company and business stage
Produce actionable deliverables including checklists, a Florida setup roadmap, and a U.S. market readiness assessment

modules.title: Welcome — What It Actually Takes
modules.description: Sets expectations before the modules begin: realistic timelines, cost ranges, what this program covers and what it doesn't. Introduces the four core themes that run throughout the course.
learningOutcomes: Describe the four core themes of the program (risk reduction, financial awareness, mistake avoidance, cultural understanding)
Identify realistic cost ranges and timelines for establishing a U.S. business

lessons.title: Before You Begin — What This Program Will (and Won't) Do for You
objectives: Describe what the U.S. Market Readiness Program covers and its limitations
Identify the four core themes (Reduce Risk, Watch Your Money, Avoid Common Mistakes, Understand the Culture)
Describe the realistic timeline (3-6 months) for establishing a U.S. business presence

contentBlocks.title: Time Commitment & Program Introduction
contentBlocks.description: This program requires less than 3 hours of self-paced learning. Practical introduction to the legal, financial, and cultural realities of operating a business in the United States.

contentBlocks.title: Legal & Educational Disclaimer
contentBlocks.description: This program provides general educational information. It does not constitute legal, tax, immigration, or financial advice. Participants should consult qualified professionals before making business decisions.

contentBlocks.title: U.S. Expansion Is a Strategy, Not a Registration
contentBlocks.description: Opening concept: Many founders think 'Form LLC → done.' But U.S. expansion requires strategy across legal, financial, cultural, and market dimensions. This program gives you the framework.

contentBlocks.title: Immigration & Visa Topics
contentBlocks.description: This program briefly touches on immigration and visa topics. Immigration law is complex and individual — consult qualified immigration experts before making any decisions about visa status.

contentBlocks.title: Four Themes You'll See Throughout This Course
contentBlocks.description: Introduces Reduce Risk, Watch Your Money, Avoid Common Mistakes, Understand the Culture — the four lenses through which every module's content is framed.

contentBlocks.title: Common Mistakes International Founders Make
contentBlocks.description: Preview of the repeating callout that appears in every module — highlighting the most frequent and costly errors founders make at each stage.

contentBlocks.title: The Real Timeline
contentBlocks.description: Typical timeline overview: 3-6 months to be fully operational. Entity formation is fast — banking, compliance, and first customers take longer. No specific cost numbers here — those appear in per-module Watch Your Money callouts.

contentBlocks.title: How to Use This Program
contentBlocks.description: Non-linear navigation explained — founders can do modules in any order. Recommendation to start with Module 1 (legal setup) but freedom to jump to what matters most. Phase 1 introduces founders to the U.S. market; companies that decide to move forward may continue into deeper advisory programs.

contentBlocks.title: Welcome Video — Avatar Host
contentBlocks.description: 60-90 second welcome video with avatar host. Program overview, what to expect, encouragement.

suggestedActivities: Self-assessment: Rate your current knowledge across the seven topic areas covered in this program
Reflection: List your top 3 questions or concerns about entering the U.S. market
Navigation exercise: Browse the module menu and identify which 2-3 modules are most relevant to your current situation

modules.title: Getting Your Foot in the Door
modules.description: Legal foundations for establishing a U.S. business entity in Florida. Covers entity selection, SunBiz registration, EIN, registered address, and a brief introduction to banking (detailed in Module 2).
learningOutcomes: Compare the three main U.S. entity types (LLC, C-Corp, S-Corp) and select the most appropriate structure for their business
Complete the Florida business registration process through SunBiz, including EIN and registered address
Identify the initial requirements and common barriers to opening a U.S. business bank account

lessons.title: Choosing Your U.S. Business Entity
objectives: Compare LLC, C-Corp, and S-Corp structures for international founders
Identify which entity type fits their business goals, tax situation, and immigration status
Estimate formation costs and timeline for each entity type

contentBlocks.title: The Three Entity Types — And Why It Matters More Than You Think
contentBlocks.description: Overview of LLC, C-Corp, S-Corp from the perspective of a non-U.S. founder. Why entity choice affects banking, taxes, visa eligibility, and investor access.

contentBlocks.title: LLC vs. C-Corp vs. S-Corp — Side by Side
contentBlocks.description: Three-column comparison card: liability protection, tax treatment, investor appeal, immigration compatibility, formation cost, complexity.

contentBlocks.title: Choosing an Entity Based on What Friends Recommend
contentBlocks.description: Why 'my friend formed an LLC so I should too' is a risky approach. Your entity type depends on YOUR tax situation, visa, and growth plans.

contentBlocks.title: Entity Formation — Cost Ranges and Timelines
contentBlocks.description: Table: LLC ($100-$500 + state fee), C-Corp ($500-$1,500 + state fee), S-Corp (C-Corp + election). Timeline: 1-5 business days online.

contentBlocks.title: Reduce Risk — Get It Right the First Time
contentBlocks.description: Changing entity type later is expensive and disruptive. Spend the time now to choose correctly.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Your entity type affects everything — taxes, banking, visas, investors. Don't default to LLC without understanding the implications for your specific situation.

suggestedActivities: Decision exercise: Based on your business type, visa status, and growth plans, identify which entity type best fits your situation
Comparison activity: Review the LLC vs. C-Corp vs. S-Corp table and note which factors matter most for your company
Checklist: List 3 questions to ask a U.S. business attorney about entity selection

lessons.title: Registering with SunBiz — Step by Step
objectives: Describe the complete SunBiz registration process for a Florida business entity
Identify the requirements for an EIN, registered agent, and registered address
Explain why a physical registered address matters and how UCF BIP can help

contentBlocks.title: SunBiz — Florida's Division of Corporations
contentBlocks.description: What SunBiz is, why Florida registration is your first concrete step, what you need before you start the process.

contentBlocks.title: Registration Timeline — From Zero to Entity
contentBlocks.description: Step-by-step timeline: 1) Choose entity type, 2) Name availability check, 3) File formation docs on SunBiz, 4) Designate registered agent, 5) Apply for EIN, 6) Receive confirmation. Estimated: 1-5 business days.

contentBlocks.title: EIN — Your Business's Social Security Number
contentBlocks.description: What an EIN is, why you need it for banking and taxes, how to apply (IRS online — free, immediate for U.S.-based applicants; SS-4 form for international).

contentBlocks.title: Registered Agent and Address — The Physical Presence Requirement
contentBlocks.description: Why Florida requires a physical registered address (not P.O. box), what a registered agent does, UCF BIP flexible office space as an option.

contentBlocks.title: Common Filing Errors That Delay Your Registration
contentBlocks.description: Name already taken, wrong entity suffix, missing registered agent designation, incorrect principal address format.

contentBlocks.title: Before You Pay Anyone to File for You
contentBlocks.description: SunBiz filing is simple and inexpensive ($125 for LLC, $70 for Corp). Many founders pay $500-$2,000 to agents for something they can do in 30 minutes.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Florida registration is fast and affordable. The complexity isn't in filing — it's in choosing the right entity type, registered agent, and address before you file.

suggestedActivities: Walkthrough: Visit SunBiz.org and review the filing requirements for your chosen entity type
Checklist: Gather the documents needed for SunBiz registration (name, address, registered agent, articles)
Planning exercise: Determine your registered address strategy — registered agent service, UCF BIP office, or other option

lessons.title: Opening a U.S. Bank Account — The First Hurdle
objectives: List the documents required to open a U.S. business bank account as a foreign founder
Identify common reasons bank applications get rejected
Describe the difference between traditional banking and payment processors (Stripe, Square)

contentBlocks.title: Why Banking Is Harder Than You Expect
contentBlocks.description: International founders consistently rank banking as their biggest surprise. Physical presence requirements, document expectations, and the reality that not every bank will work with you.

contentBlocks.title: Walking Into a Bank Without the Right Documents
contentBlocks.description: The documents you need are specific — and most banks won't tell you upfront what's missing. Arrive prepared or expect to make multiple trips.

contentBlocks.title: What You'll Typically Need
contentBlocks.description: Document checklist: EIN letter, Articles of Organization/Incorporation, Operating Agreement (LLC), passport, U.S. address proof, initial deposit.

contentBlocks.title: Payment Processors — An Alternative Starting Point
contentBlocks.description: Stripe, Square, and PayPal as interim solutions while banking gets sorted. Foreign founder limitations with each. Why this isn't a replacement for a real bank account.

contentBlocks.title: Watch Your Money — Bank Account Fees Add Up
contentBlocks.description: Monthly fees, wire transfer costs, minimum balance requirements. Compare before you commit.

contentBlocks.title: We'll Go Deeper in Module 2
contentBlocks.description: Brief bridge to Module 2's banking deep dive — credit history, account rejections, realistic timelines.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Banking is the biggest friction point for international founders. Prepare your documents before you walk in, consider payment processors as a bridge, and expect the process to take 2-4 weeks.

suggestedActivities: Preparation checklist: Compile the documents U.S. banks typically require from international founders
Research activity: Identify 2-3 banks in the Orlando/Central Florida area that work with international business owners
Action item: Determine whether you need an ITIN or SSN before opening a business bank account

modules.title: Watch Your Money
modules.description: Taxes, accounting, and the banking deep dive. Federal and state tax obligations, sales tax, bookkeeping expectations, and the detailed reality of opening and maintaining a U.S. bank account as a foreign founder.
learningOutcomes: Explain federal and state tax obligations for foreign-owned U.S. entities
Describe sales tax collection requirements and basic accounting/bookkeeping expectations
Navigate the full banking process including document requirements, common rejections, credit history, and realistic timelines

lessons.title: Federal and State Taxes — What You Actually Owe
objectives: Distinguish between federal and Florida state tax obligations for foreign-owned entities
Identify key IRS filing requirements including ITIN vs. SSN for foreign founders
Explain how entity type affects your tax treatment (pass-through vs. corporate)

contentBlocks.title: The Good News: Florida Has No State Income Tax
contentBlocks.description: Florida's tax advantage for business. But federal taxes still apply — and they're more complex than most international founders expect.

contentBlocks.title: Federal vs. State vs. Local — What Applies to You
contentBlocks.description: Tabbed breakdown: Federal (IRS: income tax, self-employment, payroll), State (FL: no income tax, corporate income tax for C-Corps), Local (county/city business licenses, tangible personal property tax).

contentBlocks.title: ITIN, SSN, and Why Your Tax ID Matters
contentBlocks.description: When you need an ITIN vs. SSN. How to get an ITIN as a non-resident. Why some founders get caught without the right tax ID.

contentBlocks.title: The Tax Surprise That Catches Every Founder
contentBlocks.description: Estimated quarterly tax payments. Most international founders don't know about them until they owe penalties.

contentBlocks.title: Tax Treaties — Check Before You Assume
contentBlocks.description: Your home country may have a tax treaty with the U.S. that changes your obligations. Check with a CPA who handles international clients.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Florida's lack of state income tax is real — but federal obligations, quarterly estimates, and payroll taxes still apply. Budget for a CPA in your first year.

suggestedActivities: Calculation exercise: Estimate your first-year U.S. tax obligations based on your expected revenue and entity type
Checklist: Identify which federal and Florida state tax filings apply to your business
Research activity: Find a CPA in Central Florida who has experience with international business clients

lessons.title: Sales Tax, Accounting, and Keeping the Books
objectives: Explain Florida sales tax collection and remittance requirements
Describe basic U.S. bookkeeping expectations and why they differ from home-country practices
Estimate annual accounting costs for a small foreign-owned business

contentBlocks.title: Florida Sales Tax — It's Not Optional
contentBlocks.description: 6% state rate + county surtax. Which products and services are taxable. How to register for a sales tax permit with the Florida Department of Revenue.

contentBlocks.title: Collecting Sales Tax Without a Permit
contentBlocks.description: Some founders collect sales tax before registering — that's a compliance violation. Others don't collect at all — that's a liability. Register first, then collect.

contentBlocks.title: U.S. Bookkeeping Expectations
contentBlocks.description: GAAP basics for small businesses. Why U.S. banks, investors, and the IRS expect a certain standard. QuickBooks, Xero, and other tools.

contentBlocks.title: Annual Accounting Costs — What to Budget
contentBlocks.description: Table: Bookkeeping software ($20-$60/mo), CPA for tax prep ($500-$2,000/yr), sales tax filing (often included), payroll service ($40-$100/mo if hiring).

contentBlocks.title: Before You Hire an Accountant
contentBlocks.description: Find a CPA who works with foreign-owned entities. Not all do. Ask about treaty experience, ITIN filing, and multi-country income.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Sales tax compliance is mandatory and easy to mess up. Budget for a CPA who understands international clients — it pays for itself in avoided penalties.

suggestedActivities: Comparison activity: Map how U.S. sales tax differs from the VAT system in your home country
Checklist: Determine whether your product or service is subject to Florida sales tax
Planning exercise: Outline a basic bookkeeping system for your U.S. operations

lessons.title: Banking Deep Dive — The Reality for International Founders
objectives: Identify the physical presence and documentation requirements for major U.S. banks
Explain why bank accounts get rejected and how to improve approval chances
Describe U.S. credit history requirements and realistic timelines for building credit

contentBlocks.title: Why Banks Say No — And What to Do About It
contentBlocks.description: Common rejection reasons: no U.S. credit history, incomplete documentation, entity type mismatch, high-risk country flags. Which banks are more international-friendly.

contentBlocks.title: Reality Check: This Takes Longer Than You Think
contentBlocks.description: First bank account: 2-4 weeks if everything goes right. Credit card: 3-6 months. Credit line: 12-18 months. Plan accordingly.

contentBlocks.title: Building U.S. Credit History From Zero
contentBlocks.description: Secured credit cards, business credit cards that don't require SSN, credit-builder accounts. Why U.S. credit history matters for everything — office leases, vendor terms, future financing.

contentBlocks.title: Payment Processors, Wire Transfers, and Moving Money
contentBlocks.description: Stripe and Square foreign founder limitations. International wire transfer costs and timing. Wise (TransferWise) as a bridge. ACH vs. wire vs. check in U.S. business.

contentBlocks.title: Watch Your Money — Wire Transfer Fees Are Real
contentBlocks.description: International wires cost $25-$50 each direction. ACH is free or low-cost but domestic only. Plan your money movement strategy.

contentBlocks.title: Latin America Resources — Banking
contentBlocks.description: Banks with Spanish-speaking staff in Central Florida. Hispanic Chamber banking workshops. SBDC Orlando financial counseling.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Banking is the #1 friction point for international founders. Come with every document ready, apply to international-friendly banks, and start building U.S. credit from day one.

suggestedActivities: Reality check: Review common bank account rejection reasons and assess your current readiness
Research activity: Compare fee structures of 3 banks that serve international founders in Central Florida
Self-assessment: Evaluate your funding readiness — can you bootstrap, or do you need investor/loan options?

modules.title: Protecting What You're Building
modules.description: Intellectual property, contracts, insurance, and knowing when to bring in a lawyer. How to protect your business assets in the U.S. legal system without overspending.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish between copyright, trademark, and patent protections and identify which apply to their business
Describe key contract, insurance, and regulatory compliance requirements
Assess when professional legal counsel is needed and estimate associated costs

lessons.title: U.S. Intellectual Property — Copyright, Trademark, Patent
objectives: Distinguish between copyright, trademark, and patent protections in the U.S.
Identify which IP protections apply to their business assets
Estimate filing costs and timelines for trademark and patent applications

contentBlocks.title: Three Types of IP Protection — And Which Ones You Actually Need
contentBlocks.description: Copyright (automatic but register for enforcement), Trademark (your brand identity — register with USPTO), Patent (inventions — expensive and slow). Most international founders need trademark first.

contentBlocks.title: Copyright vs. Trademark vs. Patent
contentBlocks.description: Three-column comparison: what it protects, how long it lasts, cost to file, timeline, do-you-need-a-lawyer.

contentBlocks.title: Assuming Your Home Country IP Protects You in the U.S.
contentBlocks.description: IP protection is territorial. Your trademark in Brazil, Germany, or Colombia means nothing in the U.S. You must file separately.

contentBlocks.title: IP Protection — Costs and Timelines
contentBlocks.description: Table: Trademark ($250-$350 per class, 8-12 months), Patent ($5,000-$15,000+, 2-3 years), Copyright ($45-$65, 3-6 months), NDA drafting ($200-$500).

contentBlocks.title: Reduce Risk — Register Your Trademark Before You Launch
contentBlocks.description: If someone else files your brand name in the U.S. first, you may lose it. File early, even if it takes months to process.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Trademark your brand name before you invest in U.S. marketing. IP protection is territorial — what you have at home doesn't follow you here.

suggestedActivities: IP audit: List all intellectual property your company owns and determine which protections apply in the U.S.
Comparison activity: Review the differences between copyright, trademark, and patent protection for your business assets
Action item: Check the USPTO database for potential trademark conflicts with your brand name in the U.S.

lessons.title: Contracts, Insurance, and Regulatory Exposure
objectives: Identify essential contract types for U.S. business operations (NDAs, service agreements, terms of service)
Describe basic business insurance requirements and options
Explain key regulatory exposure areas for international founders

contentBlocks.title: Contracts in the U.S. — Everything Gets Written Down
contentBlocks.description: U.S. business culture is contract-heavy. Verbal agreements carry little weight. What you need: NDA, service agreement, terms of service, operating agreement. What each one does.

contentBlocks.title: Culture Shift — The Handshake Deal Is Dead
contentBlocks.description: In many countries, business relationships run on trust and handshakes. In the U.S., the contract IS the relationship. This isn't distrust — it's how business operates.

contentBlocks.title: Business Insurance — What You Need and What Can Wait
contentBlocks.description: General liability, professional liability (E&O), workers' comp (required if hiring), commercial property. Costs and what's actually required vs. nice-to-have.

contentBlocks.title: Operating Without General Liability Insurance
contentBlocks.description: One slip-and-fall, one product issue, one bad contract — and you're personally exposed if your LLC doesn't have GL coverage.

contentBlocks.title: Regulatory Exposure — Industries That Need Extra Attention
contentBlocks.description: Food/beverage (FDA, FDACS), healthcare (HIPAA), financial services (SEC/FINRA), children's products (CPSC). If you're in a regulated industry, budget for compliance.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Get your contracts right, carry basic insurance, and know if your industry has specific regulatory requirements. These are costs — but they're cheaper than lawsuits.

suggestedActivities: Checklist: Identify which types of business insurance your U.S. operations will need
Research activity: Get preliminary quotes for general liability and professional liability insurance
Risk assessment: List your top 3 business risks in the U.S. and match each to an insurance or legal protection

lessons.title: When to Bring In a Lawyer — And What It Costs
objectives: Identify situations that require professional legal counsel vs. what you can handle yourself
Estimate legal costs for common business needs (entity formation, contracts, trademark, disputes)
Describe how to find and evaluate a business attorney for international founders

contentBlocks.title: You Don't Need a Lawyer for Everything
contentBlocks.description: What you can do yourself (SunBiz filing, EIN, basic contracts via templates) vs. what needs a lawyer (complex IP, immigration-linked business, investor agreements, disputes).

contentBlocks.title: Legal Costs — Realistic Ranges
contentBlocks.description: Table: Initial consultation ($0-$300), contract review ($200-$500), entity formation with attorney ($500-$1,500), trademark filing ($500-$1,000), immigration-business combo ($2,000-$10,000+).

contentBlocks.title: Before You Hire a Lawyer
contentBlocks.description: Get a free SBDC consultation first. Check if UCF BIP has legal workshop partnerships. Ask for flat-fee quotes, not hourly. Verify they work with international clients.

contentBlocks.title: Finding the Right Attorney
contentBlocks.description: Business attorney vs. immigration attorney vs. IP attorney — they're different specialties. Florida Bar referral service. SBDC legal workshops. Questions to ask before hiring.

contentBlocks.title: Latin America Resources — Legal
contentBlocks.description: Bilingual attorneys in Central Florida who specialize in international business. Hispanic Bar Association. Prospera legal workshops.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Use free resources first (SBDC, UCF BIP workshops), handle simple filings yourself, and save attorney hours for the decisions that really need them.

suggestedActivities: Contract review: Identify which standard U.S. business contracts you will need in your first 6 months
Checklist: Create a list of criteria for selecting a U.S. business attorney
Action item: Research attorney referral resources (Florida Bar, SBDC, UCF BIP network)

modules.title: Building Your Team
modules.description: Higher-level overview of hiring your first U.S. employees, the contractor vs. employee distinction, visa pathway awareness for founders, and employer compliance essentials. Streamlined per client feedback.
learningOutcomes: Distinguish between contractor and employee classifications and the legal consequences of misclassification
Compare visa pathways (O-1, E-2, L-1) and assess which are realistic for their situation
Describe basic payroll, employer tax, and compliance obligations

lessons.title: Your First U.S. Hire — Contractor vs. Employee
objectives: Distinguish between independent contractor and employee classifications under U.S. law
Identify the financial and legal penalties for worker misclassification
Describe the IRS factors used to determine worker classification

contentBlocks.title: The #1 Mistake International Founders Make When Hiring
contentBlocks.description: Calling someone a 'contractor' when they're legally an employee. Why this matters: back taxes, penalties, benefits owed. The IRS doesn't care what your contract says — they care how the person actually works.

contentBlocks.title: Contractor vs. Employee — Key Differences
contentBlocks.description: Side-by-side comparison: control over work, payment structure, benefits, tax withholding, termination, legal exposure.

contentBlocks.title: Reduce Risk — The IRS ABC Test
contentBlocks.description: Three factors the IRS uses: behavioral control, financial control, relationship type. If you control WHEN, WHERE, and HOW someone works — they're an employee.

contentBlocks.title: Using a 1099 to Avoid Payroll Taxes
contentBlocks.description: Many founders try to save money by classifying everyone as 1099 contractors. The IRS actively audits this — penalties include back taxes, interest, and fines up to $50,000.

contentBlocks.title: When Contractors Make Sense
contentBlocks.description: Project-based work, specialized skills, short-term needs. Using platforms like Upwork, Toptal. Proper contractor agreements.

contentBlocks.title: UCF Talent Pipeline & Local Hiring
contentBlocks.description: University of Central Florida: 70,000+ students, internship programs, local talent pipeline. Local hiring cost ranges in Central Florida compared to other U.S. markets.

contentBlocks.title: Common Mistakes International Founders Make — Hiring
contentBlocks.description: Misclassifying employees as contractors, not understanding true cost of hiring (salary + 15-30% overhead), working without proper visa authorization.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Get worker classification right from day one. The cost of misclassification far exceeds the cost of doing it correctly. When in doubt, consult an employment attorney.

suggestedActivities: Decision framework: Determine whether your first U.S. hire should be a contractor or employee based on your needs
Cost calculation: Estimate the true cost of your first employee (salary + 15-30% overhead)
Research activity: Explore UCF talent pipeline resources and internship programs available through the university

lessons.title: Visa Pathways — What's Actually Realistic
objectives: Compare visa pathways that may be available (O-1, E-2, L-1, H-1B) — all visa and immigration actions should be evaluated with an immigration attorney
Assess which visa pathway is realistic based on their specific situation
Estimate visa application costs and processing timelines

contentBlocks.title: Important Disclaimer
contentBlocks.description: Completing this program does not influence or guarantee visa approval. This content is educational only — visa outcomes depend on individual circumstances and are determined solely by U.S. immigration authorities.

contentBlocks.title: The Visa Landscape for International Founders
contentBlocks.description: Awareness-level overview of common visa paths. Emphasize that all visa and immigration actions should be evaluated with an immigration attorney. Streamlined — focus on awareness, not procedural how-to.

contentBlocks.title: Visa Pathways — Detailed Breakdown
contentBlocks.description: Expandable sections: O-1 (extraordinary ability — for proven track records), E-2 (treaty investor — requires substantial investment), L-1 (intracompany transfer — for existing companies), H-1B (specialty occupation — lottery-based, employer-sponsored). Each with: requirements, costs, timeline, realistic assessment.

contentBlocks.title: Reality Check: Visa Processing Takes Time
contentBlocks.description: Standard E-2 processing: 4-8 months. O-1 premium processing: 2-4 weeks but expensive ($2,805 premium fee). H-1B lottery: annual, ~25% acceptance rate. Plan 6-12 months ahead.

contentBlocks.title: Working Without Proper Authorization
contentBlocks.description: Even as a business owner, your visa status determines what work you can do. Working outside your authorization can result in deportation and permanent bars.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Start your visa process early, be realistic about timelines, and work with an immigration attorney who is honest about your chances — not just optimistic.

suggestedActivities: Self-assessment: Review the visa pathway comparison and identify which options may apply to your situation
Checklist: List the key questions to ask an immigration attorney about your specific visa options
Planning exercise: Create a timeline for any visa-related actions needed for your U.S. business activities

lessons.title: Payroll, Taxes, and Employer Obligations
objectives: Describe basic payroll setup requirements and employer tax obligations
Identify required employer-provided protections (workers' comp, unemployment insurance)
Estimate the true cost of an employee beyond their salary

contentBlocks.title: The True Cost of an Employee
contentBlocks.description: Salary is just the start. Add: employer FICA (7.65%), FUTA, state unemployment, workers' comp, benefits. A $50K employee actually costs $57K-$65K+.

contentBlocks.title: Employer Cost Breakdown
contentBlocks.description: Table: Base salary, + FICA (7.65%), + FUTA (~$420/yr), + FL state unemployment (~$18-$378/yr), + workers' comp (varies by industry), + benefits (optional but expected). Total: salary x 1.15-1.30.

contentBlocks.title: Payroll Services — Don't Do It Yourself
contentBlocks.description: Why DIY payroll is a bad idea for international founders. Services like Gusto, ADP, Paychex handle withholding, filing, and compliance. Costs $40-$100/mo + per-employee fees.

contentBlocks.title: Watch Your Money — Payroll Tax Penalties Are Serious
contentBlocks.description: The IRS treats unpaid payroll taxes as a 'trust fund' violation — one of the few tax penalties that can't be discharged in bankruptcy. Don't mess this up.

contentBlocks.title: What U.S. Employees Expect
contentBlocks.description: PTO, sick leave, health benefits — not always legally required for small businesses but culturally expected. Not offering them limits your hiring pool.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: An employee costs 15-30% more than their salary. Use a payroll service from day one and budget for the full employer cost before making your first hire.

suggestedActivities: Research activity: Compare 2-3 payroll service providers and their pricing for small businesses
Checklist: Review employer tax obligations (FICA, FUTA, state unemployment) and estimated costs
Action item: Determine your payroll schedule and identify which tax withholdings apply to your situation

modules.title: Understanding U.S. Customers
modules.description: Why products and services rarely transfer directly to the U.S. market. Customer acquisition, pricing, sales cycles, and vendor onboarding requirements.
learningOutcomes: Explain why products and services often require adaptation for the U.S. market
Apply U.S. customer acquisition strategies and pricing models to their business

lessons.title: Why Your Product Probably Needs to Change
objectives: Explain why successful products in other markets often fail when transferred directly to the U.S.
Identify the key areas where U.S. market adaptation is typically needed (pricing, packaging, messaging, compliance)
Describe the concept of product-market fit in the U.S. context

contentBlocks.title: What Works at Home Doesn't Automatically Work Here
contentBlocks.description: Common assumption: 'My product is successful — I just need to sell it in the U.S.' Reality: pricing expectations, packaging standards, competitive landscape, regulatory requirements, and customer expectations are all different.

contentBlocks.title: Launching With Your Home Market Price
contentBlocks.description: Pricing that works in Colombia or Germany often doesn't work in the U.S. — sometimes too low (perceived as cheap/unreliable), sometimes too high (established competitors). Research your U.S. competitive set.

contentBlocks.title: Adaptation, Not Translation
contentBlocks.description: Market entry isn't about translating your website. It's about understanding U.S. buyer psychology, competitive positioning, compliance requirements, and go-to-market channels.

contentBlocks.title: U.S. Buyers Value Different Things
contentBlocks.description: Speed, convenience, customer service, social proof (reviews), money-back guarantees. These aren't optional extras — they're baseline expectations.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Plan to adapt your product, pricing, and messaging for the U.S. market. The founders who succeed here are the ones who listen to U.S. customers before they launch — not after.

suggestedActivities: Market adaptation exercise: Identify 3 specific ways your product or service needs to change for U.S. customers
Pricing analysis: Research competitor pricing in your U.S. target market and compare to your current pricing
Customer persona: Create a profile of your ideal U.S. customer including their expectations and buying behavior

lessons.title: U.S. Customer Acquisition — How Americans Buy
objectives: Describe common U.S. customer acquisition channels (digital, referral, direct, partnerships)
Explain U.S. sales cycle expectations including timeline, follow-up norms, and closing patterns
Identify pricing strategy considerations for the U.S. market

contentBlocks.title: Customer Acquisition in the U.S.
contentBlocks.description: Digital-first market: SEO, content marketing, paid ads, social media. B2B: LinkedIn, trade shows, referral networks. B2C: Amazon, direct-to-consumer, influencer marketing. What works depends on your business model.

contentBlocks.title: The U.S. Sales Cycle — Faster and More Direct
contentBlocks.description: U.S. buyers expect quick responses (same day), clear pricing (no hidden fees), and direct communication. Long relationship-building before a sale is rare in most sectors. Time is money — literally.

contentBlocks.title: Not Following Up Fast Enough
contentBlocks.description: In many countries, a 3-day response to an inquiry is normal. In the U.S., if you don't respond within hours, they've already contacted your competitor.

contentBlocks.title: Scenario: A U.S. Customer Wants a Quote
contentBlocks.description: Decision point scenario: You receive an inquiry from a potential U.S. customer. Walk through the expected response timeline, pricing presentation, and follow-up sequence.

contentBlocks.title: Vendor Onboarding — What U.S. Companies Require From You
contentBlocks.description: When selling to U.S. companies (especially B2B), founders must understand vendor onboarding: W-9 forms (tax identification), insurance certificates (proof of liability coverage), and procurement processes. Founders rarely know about these requirements and get blindsided.

contentBlocks.title: Common Mistakes International Founders Make — Sales
contentBlocks.description: Launching with home-market pricing, not following up fast enough on inquiries, skipping vendor onboarding requirements.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Speed wins in the U.S. market. Respond fast, price transparently, follow up consistently, and be ready for vendor onboarding requirements.

suggestedActivities: Sales channel mapping: Identify the 2-3 most promising customer acquisition channels for your business in the U.S.
Vendor readiness checklist: Prepare your W-9, insurance certificates, and company credentials for B2B sales
Outreach plan: Draft a 30-day customer acquisition plan with specific tactics and targets

modules.title: How Business Actually Works Here
modules.description: U.S. business culture from the international founder's perspective. Communication norms, meeting etiquette, negotiation patterns, trust signals, and the cultural missteps that cost real money.
learningOutcomes: Explain key U.S. business communication norms including directness, speed, and trust-building patterns
Apply U.S. meeting etiquette, negotiation practices, and follow-through expectations to business interactions
Identify common cultural missteps and their business consequences

lessons.title: Speed, Directness, and Trust Signals
objectives: Explain how U.S. business communication differs from other cultures (directness, brevity, time-sensitivity)
Identify the trust signals American business partners look for (responsiveness, follow-through, transparency)
Describe how to build credibility as an international founder in U.S. business settings

contentBlocks.title: Americans Mean What They Say — Literally
contentBlocks.description: Low-context communication: direct, specific, action-oriented. 'Let's circle back' means you'll hear from them. 'That's interesting' might mean they're not interested. How to read and use U.S. business language.

contentBlocks.title: Speed Equals Professionalism
contentBlocks.description: In U.S. business, response time is a trust signal. Slow responses = unreliable. Same-day email replies, quick meeting scheduling, and prompt follow-up build credibility faster than a great pitch deck.

contentBlocks.title: Building Trust as an Outsider
contentBlocks.description: Trust in U.S. business comes from delivery, not relationships. You build credibility by doing what you said you'd do, when you said you'd do it. Repeat that enough times and relationships follow.

contentBlocks.title: Over-Investing in Relationship Before Business
contentBlocks.description: Many cultures invest heavily in personal relationships before discussing business. In the U.S., the business comes first. Long social preambles can be seen as unfocused or wasting time.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Be direct, be fast, and follow through. Trust in U.S. business is earned through action — not titles, credentials, or personal connections.

suggestedActivities: Cultural comparison: List 3 business communication norms in your home country that differ from U.S. expectations
Practice exercise: Draft a professional follow-up email after a hypothetical first meeting with a U.S. partner
Observation activity: Identify examples of direct vs. indirect communication styles in U.S. business contexts

lessons.title: Meeting Etiquette, Negotiation, and Follow-Through
objectives: Apply U.S. meeting etiquette norms (punctuality, agenda-driven, action items)
Describe U.S. negotiation patterns and how they differ from other business cultures
Explain the critical importance of written follow-up in U.S. business

contentBlocks.title: Meetings — Time Is the Currency
contentBlocks.description: Start on time, end on time, have an agenda, leave with action items. 30-minute meetings are normal. Being 5 minutes late signals disrespect. Sending a recap email within 24 hours is expected, not optional.

contentBlocks.title: The Recap Email You're Not Sending
contentBlocks.description: After every meeting, send a short email: what was discussed, what was agreed, next steps, deadlines. In the U.S., if it's not in writing, it didn't happen.

contentBlocks.title: Negotiation — More Direct Than You're Used To
contentBlocks.description: U.S. negotiation is straightforward: both sides state their position, find the middle, close quickly. Prolonged negotiation is a red flag. First offers are usually close to final. Counter-offers are expected but limited (1-2 rounds).

contentBlocks.title: Scenario: The Meeting After the Meeting
contentBlocks.description: Decision scenario: Your meeting went well. What happens in the next 24 hours determines whether the deal moves forward. Walk through the expected follow-up sequence.

contentBlocks.title: The Follow-Through Test
contentBlocks.description: Americans test new business partners with small commitments: 'Send me that by Friday.' If you deliver, trust builds. If you miss it, the relationship stalls. Every deadline is a test.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Be punctual, agenda-driven, and follow up in writing within 24 hours. Every small commitment you keep builds trust for the bigger ones.

suggestedActivities: Networking preparation: Create your 60-second elevator pitch tailored for a U.S. business audience
Meeting simulation: Plan the agenda and follow-up actions for a first meeting with a potential U.S. partner
Self-reflection: Identify your personal negotiation style and how it may need to adapt for U.S. business culture

lessons.title: Cultural Missteps That Cost Real Money
objectives: Identify the most common cultural missteps international founders make in U.S. business
Explain how cultural miscommunication affects trust, deals, and partnerships
Apply specific strategies to avoid cultural friction in U.S. business interactions

contentBlocks.title: Real Stories From Real Founders
contentBlocks.description: Anonymized examples: The founder who brought expensive gifts (seen as bribery). The founder who avoided saying 'no' (misread as agreement). The founder who spent weeks building rapport before asking for business (lost the deal to a faster competitor).

contentBlocks.title: U.S. Business Phrases — What They Really Mean
contentBlocks.description: Flashcards: 'Let's circle back' (= probably not happening), 'That's interesting' (= polite deflection), 'Let me check with my team' (= soft no or stalling), 'Let's put a pin in it' (= topic is dead), 'We should get coffee sometime' (= don't wait for the invite).

contentBlocks.title: Confusing Friendliness With Commitment
contentBlocks.description: Americans are friendly in business settings — it's cultural, not personal. A warm lunch meeting doesn't mean they'll buy from you. Read the follow-up actions, not the smiles.

contentBlocks.title: Investment Culture and Fundraising
contentBlocks.description: U.S. investor expectations: traction, market size, team, pitch deck. Getting money is not easy — even for Americans. For international founders, it's harder. Tease Phase 2 investor readiness resources.

contentBlocks.title: Latin America Resources — Cultural Bridge
contentBlocks.description: Hispanic Chamber networking events. Prospera mentor matching. SBDC Orlando bilingual business counseling. Cultural bridge workshops.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Cultural missteps are expensive. Learn the unwritten rules, read actions over words, and remember: in U.S. business, friendliness and commitment are two different things.

suggestedActivities: Case study analysis: Review the cultural misstep examples and identify which risks are most relevant to your situation
Prevention checklist: Create a personal list of cultural do/dont rules for your first 90 days of U.S. business operations
Cost awareness exercise: Estimate the potential financial impact of cultural missteps in your industry

modules.title: You're Not Alone — Central Florida's Business Support Ecosystem
modules.description: The Central Florida ecosystem offers advantages designed to help international founders succeed — strong infrastructure, lower cost than many comparable U.S. markets, and international connectivity that few other regions can match. Key organizations, why Central Florida, UCF BIP programs, and community resources.
learningOutcomes: Identify the key organizations and resources available to international founders in Central Florida
Identify Central Florida industry clusters and market opportunities relevant to their sector
Explain Central Florida's unique advantages for international business (industry clusters, LATAM connectivity, cost)
Describe UCF BIP phases 2 and 3 of the market entry/soft landing program and how to access them

lessons.title: Key Organizations — Business Support Available to Help You
objectives: Identify SBDC, SBA, chambers of commerce, and economic development agencies relevant to their business
Describe what each organization offers (counseling, funding access, networking, training)
Explain how to access these resources and what to expect from the engagement

contentBlocks.title: You Don't Have to Figure This Out Alone
contentBlocks.description: Most international founders don't know these resources exist — or assume they're only for Americans. Every organization listed here is free or low-cost, and most actively welcome international entrepreneurs.

contentBlocks.title: Resource Directory — What Each Organization Does
contentBlocks.description: Expandable sections: SBDC Orlando (free business counseling, workshops, market research), SBA (loans, disaster relief, government contracting), Orlando Economic Partnership (relocation support, industry connections), Orange County Economic Development (local incentives, permits), Florida SBDC Network (statewide resources).

contentBlocks.title: Watch Your Money — These Resources Are Free
contentBlocks.description: SBDC counseling: free. SBA resources: free. Chamber memberships: $200-$500/yr but often worth it for networking and credibility. Don't pay a consultant for what you can get free.

contentBlocks.title: Before You Hire a Business Consultant
contentBlocks.description: Start with free SBDC counseling. Many consultants charge thousands for advice you can get from trained SBDC advisors at no cost.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Central Florida has an entire ecosystem built to help businesses succeed — and most of it is free. Use it before you spend money on consultants.

suggestedActivities: Resource matching: Identify which support organizations (SBDC, SBA, chambers) offer services most relevant to your needs
Action item: Schedule an appointment with at least one free business counseling resource in Central Florida
Networking plan: List 3 business events or organizations you will connect with in your first month

lessons.title: Central Florida — Your Market Opportunity
objectives: Identify major Central Florida industry clusters and their relevance to international founders
Describe Central Florida's strategic advantages (LATAM trade connectivity, cost-of-living, talent pipeline)
Compare Central Florida's business environment to other U.S. entry points (Miami, NYC, Silicon Valley)

contentBlocks.title: Why Central Florida — Not Just Theme Parks
contentBlocks.description: Industry clusters: simulation/training, aerospace/defense, life sciences, fintech, AgTech, hospitality technology. $150B+ regional GDP. Growing international business community.

contentBlocks.title: Central Florida vs. Other U.S. Entry Points
contentBlocks.description: Tabbed comparison: Central Florida vs. Miami vs. NYC vs. Silicon Valley vs. Austin. Cost of living, business costs, industry focus, international community, quality of life.

contentBlocks.title: LATAM Trade Connectivity
contentBlocks.description: Orlando International Airport direct flights to 30+ Latin American cities. CFITO trade delegations. Hispanic Chamber partnerships. Central Florida as the gateway between Latin America and the U.S. market.

contentBlocks.title: Latin America Resources — Market Entry
contentBlocks.description: CFITO trade delegation calendar. Hispanic Chamber business matchmaking. Prospera market research support. SelectFlorida international business resources.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Central Florida offers lower costs, strong LATAM connectivity, and growing tech clusters — a compelling alternative to Miami or Silicon Valley for international founders.

suggestedActivities: Market analysis: Compare Central Florida industry clusters to your business sector and identify opportunities
Cost comparison: Review the cost-of-doing-business comparison and note how Central Florida compares to your alternatives
Connection mapping: Identify LATAM trade connections and logistics advantages relevant to your business

lessons.title: Why Central Florida — The Data Behind the Choice
objectives: Describe Central Florida's major industry clusters and economic data points
Explain the LATAM trade connectivity advantages of the Orlando region
Compare Central Florida's cost structure to other major U.S. business hubs
Compare Central Florida's business environment to other U.S. entry points (Miami, NYC, Silicon Valley)

contentBlocks.title: Central Florida by the Numbers
contentBlocks.description: Population growth, GDP, industry clusters (simulation, aerospace, life sciences, fintech, hospitality tech), university talent pipeline (UCF: 70,000+ students), international business community growth.

contentBlocks.title: Cost Comparison — Central Florida vs. Other Hubs
contentBlocks.description: Table: Office space, average salary, cost of living, state income tax, business formation cost — Central Florida vs. Miami, NYC, San Francisco, Austin.

contentBlocks.title: LATAM Gateway — Trade and Connectivity
contentBlocks.description: Orlando International Airport: 30+ direct routes to Latin America. CFITO trade delegations connect Central Florida businesses with Latin American partners. Growing Hispanic business community drives bilingual market access.

contentBlocks.title: Latin America Resources — Trade Connectivity
contentBlocks.description: CFITO upcoming trade delegations. SelectFlorida international business hub. dos.fl.gov Office of International Affairs. Orange County global investment resources.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Central Florida isn't just affordable — it's strategically positioned for international founders, especially those connecting Latin American and U.S. markets.

suggestedActivities: Data review: Examine 3 key economic indicators for Central Florida and assess how they benefit your business entry
Location comparison: Compare Central Florida vs. Miami, NYC, and Silicon Valley for your specific business needs
Decision framework: List your top 5 criteria for choosing a U.S. base and rate Central Florida against each

lessons.title: UCF BIP Programs — Your Next Step
objectives: Describe UCF BIP phases 2 and 3 of the market entry/soft landing program and who each is designed for
Identify community resources specifically for international founders (Hispanic Chamber, Prospera, CFITO)
Explain the next steps to engage with UCF BIP after completing this program

contentBlocks.title: UCF Business Incubation Program — Your Pathway
contentBlocks.description: Phases 2 and 3 of the UCF BIP market entry/soft landing program. What each phase offers: mentorship, workspace, workshops, connections, credibility. How to evaluate which phase fits your current stage.

contentBlocks.title: UCF BIP Programs — Which One Is for You?
contentBlocks.description: Tabbed breakdown of UCF BIP phases 2 and 3 of the market entry/soft landing program. Each tab with a 'this might be for you if...' checklist.

contentBlocks.title: Community Resources for International Founders
contentBlocks.description: Hispanic Chamber of Commerce Central Florida, Prospera (Hispanic entrepreneur support), CFITO trade delegations, Spanish-language business resources. Why these organizations exist and how to engage with them.

contentBlocks.title: Latin America Resources — Community
contentBlocks.description: Full directory: Hispanic Chamber (membership, events, advocacy), Prospera (mentoring, workshops, micro-lending), CFITO (trade delegations, export/import), Amway Center networking events.

contentBlocks.title: Success Stories — Companies That Benefited from UCF BIP
contentBlocks.description: [PENDING FROM BRIAN: 2-3 company names and brief case study details. Generate placeholder structure with representative examples that Brian will replace with real company details.]

contentBlocks.title: Common Mistakes International Founders Make — Resources
contentBlocks.description: Not knowing about free resources (SBDC, SBA), paying consultants for advice available through government programs, not leveraging UCF BIP and chamber connections.

contentBlocks.title: Reduce Risk — Don't Go It Alone
contentBlocks.description: The founders who succeed in the U.S. market use their support network. Join a chamber, attend SBDC workshops, connect with UCF BIP. The cost is minimal — the ROI is real.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: UCF BIP and the Central Florida ecosystem exist to help you succeed. Take the next step: schedule a 1:1 with the program team.

suggestedActivities: Program exploration: Review UCF BIP phases 2 and 3 and identify which program elements align with your next steps
Goal setting: Define 3 specific outcomes you want to achieve through UCF BIP support programs
Action item: Prepare questions for your 1-on-1 consultation about continuing with UCF BIP after this program

modules.title: Wrap-Up — Is the U.S. Right for You?
modules.description: Self-assessment reflection, resource downloads with digital credential, and clear next steps: 1:1 booking with UCF BIP team and Phase 2 overview.
learningOutcomes: Assess their personal readiness for U.S. market entry based on program content
Produce actionable deliverables including checklists, a Florida setup roadmap, and a U.S. market readiness assessment
Identify their logical next step (resources, Phase 2, or 1:1 consultation)

lessons.title: Self-Assessment — Your U.S. Market Readiness
objectives: Reflect on their U.S. market readiness across all seven module topics
Identify specific areas where they need more support or professional guidance
Assess whether U.S. market expansion is realistic for their company and business stage

contentBlocks.title: What You Now Know — That Most Founders Don't
contentBlocks.description: Quick recap of the seven modules and the four themes. The goal was never to make you an expert — it was to help you ask the right questions and avoid the expensive mistakes.

contentBlocks.title: Your Readiness Self-Assessment
contentBlocks.description: Reflection checklist covering all 7 modules: Do you know your entity type? Have you checked SunBiz for name availability? Do you have your banking documents ready? Have you identified your IP protection needs? Do you know if you need an employee or contractor? Have you researched your U.S. competitive set? Can you name 3 resources you'll contact this month?

contentBlocks.title: Where Do You Need More Help?
contentBlocks.description: Honest prompt: which areas feel clear and which feel overwhelming? That's normal. Here's how to get help with each one.

contentBlocks.title: The Biggest Risk Is Going It Alone
contentBlocks.description: You now know more than 90% of international founders who enter the U.S. market. Use that knowledge — and use the resources. Central Florida wants you to succeed.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: You don't need to have everything figured out. You need to know what to figure out first — and who can help. That's what this program gave you.

suggestedActivities: Comprehensive self-assessment: Rate your readiness across all seven module topics on a 1-5 scale
Gap analysis: Identify your top 3 knowledge gaps that need professional guidance before taking action
Priority ranking: Order your next steps by urgency and importance for your specific business situation

lessons.title: Resource Downloads & Digital Credential
objectives: Download the U.S. Market Entry Checklist, Florida Setup Roadmap, Central Florida Resource Guide, and Expansion Decision Worksheet
Understand the POK Tech digital credential and how to display it on LinkedIn

contentBlocks.title: Your Takeaway Resources
contentBlocks.description: Four downloadable resources that capture everything from this program in actionable formats. Use them as your running to-do list as you move forward.

contentBlocks.title: Resource Downloads
contentBlocks.description: Four tabs: U.S. Market Entry Checklist (module-by-module action items), Florida Setup Roadmap (step-by-step from entity to operational), Central Florida Resource Guide (organizations, contacts, programs), Expansion Decision Worksheet (structured go/no-go framework).

contentBlocks.title: Your Digital Credential
contentBlocks.description: POK Tech digital badge — the same credentialing provider used by ASU and other universities. LinkedIn-displayable credential that validates you have demonstrated understanding of key U.S. business setup, financial, legal, cultural, and market-entry concepts. Lightweight and non-credentialed — designed to signal commitment, not replace professional certification.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: These resources are yours to keep. Use the checklist as your running to-do list and share your digital credential on LinkedIn.

suggestedActivities: Resource download: Save all four resource documents (checklist, roadmap, resource guide, decision worksheet)
Credential claim: Complete the digital credential process through POK Tech for your LinkedIn profile
Personalization: Customize the U.S. Market Entry Checklist with your specific timeline and action items

lessons.title: Next Steps — Your UCF BIP Pathway
objectives: Schedule a 1:1 consultation with the UCF BIP team
Understand what phases 2 and 3 of the UCF BIP program offer
Identify their top 3 priority actions for the next 30 days

contentBlocks.title: Schedule Your 1:1 with UCF BIP
contentBlocks.description: Book a conversation with Brian Bedrick and the UCF BIP team to discuss your specific situation. This is the natural next step — bring your checklist, your questions, and your readiness assessment.

contentBlocks.title: What Comes Next
contentBlocks.description: Two tabs: Schedule 1:1 (Calendly embed to book a conversation with Brian), Phase 2 Overview (what comes next in the UCF BIP pathway — to be discussed in detail during the 1:1).

contentBlocks.title: Your Top 3 Actions for the Next 30 Days
contentBlocks.description: Prompt to identify their three highest-priority actions based on what they learned. Encourage them to be specific and time-bound.

contentBlocks.title: A Reminder
contentBlocks.description: This program provided general educational information. It is not legal, tax, immigration, or financial advice. As you take your next steps, consult qualified professionals for guidance specific to your situation.

contentBlocks.title: One Last Thing — Your Feedback Matters
contentBlocks.description: Value perception prompt and program improvement feedback. Two separate questions: 'Was this program worth your time?' and 'What would make it better?' Both feed into the admin dashboard.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.description: Your journey doesn't end here — it starts here. Schedule your 1:1, pick your top 3 actions, and take the first step this week.

suggestedActivities: Meeting preparation: Schedule your 1-on-1 meeting and prepare your top 3 discussion topics
Action plan: Write down your top 3 priority actions for the next 30 days
Reflection: Complete the program feedback to help improve the experience for future founders
